chunks.text:
SVIES: A Seven-Layer AI-Based Smart Vehicle
Intelligence and Enforcement System
Abstract—Existing
Automatic
Number
Plate
Recognition
(ANPR) systems are restricted to plate-text extraction and offer
no contextual vehicle intelligence or fraud-detection capability. This paper presents SVIES—the Smart Vehicle Intelligence
and Enforcement System—a seven-layer, end-to-end AI pipeline
designed for real-world Indian road enforcement. SVIES deploys
three custom-trained YOLOv8s models achieving plate detection
at mAP@50 of 97.7% (precision 0.988), helmet detection at
74.4% mAP@50, and vehicle classification at 64.4% mAP@50, all
trained on domain-specific Indian datasets on an NVIDIA Tesla
V100. A novel eight-mode Fake and Mismatched Plate Detection
module performs vehicle-type mismatch analysis, CMVR colorcode validation, IS 10731 font anomaly detection, duplicate
plate clone alerting, state code mismatch detection, and crossreferencing against VAHAN, PUCC, and stolen vehicle databases. OCR is delivered by a six-variant EasyOCR and Tesseract
ensemble augmented by a Groq Llama-4 Scout 17B vision
verification layer, with position-aware character correction for
all Indian plate formats including BH-series. Additional contributions include a hybrid helmet and seatbelt detector, Shapely-based
geofencing over 14 Hyderabad zones, three-tier repeated offender
intelligence, SHA-256 cryptographic evidence timestamping, and
a YOLOv5n offline edge mode for zero-internet deployments. SVIES is implemented as a fully containerised FastAPI + React 19 stack with real-time WebSocket streaming and Firebase
RBAC.
I. INTRODUCTION
Road traffic enforcement in India faces a persistent technological gap. Despite rapid urbanisation and ongoing smart-city
initiatives, the majority of vehicle monitoring relies on manual
police presence or basic ANPR cameras restricted to tollgate plate reading [1]. Crimes involving forged or mismatched
vehicle plates, uninsured vehicles, and habitual traffic violators
remain largely undetected owing to the absence of real-time,
context-aware enforcement systems [2]. According to the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways, 153,972 road fatalities
were recorded in India in 2021, with a significant proportion
attributable to helmet non-compliance, over-speeding, and
unregistered vehicles [3]. India’s VAHAN/Parivahan database
governs more than 300 million registered vehicles [4], and
plates must conform to IS 10731 character specifications and
CMVR-prescribed color codes. The technical complexity of Indian road enforcement is
compounded by the diversity of valid plate formats, regional
script variants, the recently introduced BH-series (Bharat
series) transferable plates, and inconsistent illumination at
checkpoints ranging from urban flyovers to unlit rural highways. Commercial solutions such as Genetec AutoVu are
optimised for Western plate standards and neither address
Indian regulatory compliance nor provide fraud-forensics capability.

Singh and Mishra demonstrated that camera-based
ML pipelines deployed on vehicles are vulnerable to electromagnetic interference (EMI) attacks that degrade real-time
inference accuracy [15], underscoring the need for hardened,
multi-layer enforcement architectures such as SVIES. This paper presents SVIES, a seven-layer AI pipeline that
transforms a live CCTV feed into actionable enforcement
intelligence. Primary contributions:
• Eight-mode Fake & Mismatched Plate Detection:
database verification (VAHAN, PUCC, insurance, stolen),
CMVR color forensics, IS 10731 font metrics, and spatiotemporal clone detection. • Six-variant LLM-augmented OCR: EasyOCR + Tesseract ensemble with position-aware correction and Groq
Llama-4 Scout 17B vision verification covering all Indian
plate formats including BH-series. • Hybrid helmet/seatbelt detector: YOLOv8s cascade,
YOLOv8n-pose
keypoints, and
an
Indian skin-tone
HSV/YCrCb heuristic. • Zone-weighted risk engine: 17 violation parameters,
4 alert tiers, and Shapely geofencing over 14 predefined Hyderabad zones with real-time OSM/Overpass
API loading. • Three-tier offender intelligence: automated escalation
from RTO notification to district superintendent challan
warning to ReportLab court summons generation. • SHA-256 cryptographic evidence chain for legal admissibility, dispatched via Twilio SMS and Gmail SMTP. • YOLOv5n offline edge mode with 30-day SQLite
caching and sync-on-connect for zero-internet Raspberry
Pi 4B deployments. II. RELATED WORK
ANPR and OCR. Early ANPR employed morphological
operations and Tesseract OCR for plate segmentation and character extraction [5]. CNN-based approaches using SSD and
Faster R-CNN improved detection reliability under real-world
conditions [6]. The YOLO family of single-stage detectors
subsequently became the standard for real-time ANPR owing
to their inference speed [7]. YOLOv8 by Ultralytics further
advanced the state of the art with anchor-free detection heads
and improved small-object localisation [8]. For Indian-specific
ANPR, key challenges include plate degradation due to dust,
rain, and nighttime conditions, inconsistent plate mounting
angles, and the diversity of valid plate formats across states [9]. Ensemble OCR approaches combining EasyOCR with rule-

Fig 1: Visual Abstract. based post-processing grammars have been shown to outperform single-engine pipelines on regional plate datasets [10]. Safety and Geofencing. Helmet detection using deep
learning has been studied independently, with YOLOv5 and
EfficientNet-B0 classifiers achieving above 90% accuracy on
curated datasets [11]. Seatbelt detection via pose-estimation
keypoints in four-wheelers has been reported [12]. Geofencing
for smart-city traffic management has been explored using
GPS-based vehicle tracking [13]. Adversarial Hardening. Singh et al. showed that EMI
injected onto vehicular camera sensors significantly reduces
the accuracy and frame rates of live ML object detection
models [15]. Complementary work on TinyML-based IoT
sensor hardening [16] and wearable IoBT platforms [17]
demonstrates that edge intelligence combined with sensorlevel protection is essential for mission-critical enforcement
deployments [18]. To the best of the authors’ knowledge,
SVIES is the first system to unify fake plate forensics, multimodal safety detection, live database intelligence, repeated offender escalation, cryptographic evidence integrity, and offline
edge capability in a single deployable platform. III. SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE
SVIES is structured as a seven-layer sequential pipeline
wherein each layer processes the output of the preceding layer
and all layers collectively contribute to a unified per-vehicle
risk profile. The complete architecture is presented in Fig. 1.
A. Layer 1: Video Capture and Preprocessing
The system accepts live CCTV or IP camera input at
a minimum resolution of 1080p at 25–30 FPS through
modules/detector.py. Adaptive illumination normalisation applies Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation
(CLAHE) on the L-channel of the LAB color space with a
clip limit of 2.0 and tile grid size of 8×8, ensuring consistent
detection quality under nighttime and adverse weather conditions. ROI masking restricts computation to lane-level vehicle
zones, reducing per-frame inference load by approximately
35% compared to full-frame processing. B. Layer 2: YOLOv8s Detection Engine
Three custom-trained YOLOv8s models (11.2M parameters
each, 640×640 input) operate in parallel per frame. The plate
detector (svies_plate_detector.pt), trained on 2,101
merged Indian plate images, localises license plate bounding
boxes at confidence threshold 0.25. The vehicle classifier
(svies_vehicle_classifier.pt), trained on 12,871
images, identifies 11 Indian-specific vehicle types including
AUTO, E RICKSHAW, TEMPO, and TRACTOR—categories
absent from standard COCO-class detectors. The ResNet50
age classifier is trained on 8,400 images across five age categories (pre-2000, 2000–2010, 2010–2015, 2015–2020, post2020), enabling age-based regulatory checks such as fitness
certificate expiry validation. C. Layer 2.5: Fake and Mismatched Plate Detection
Implemented in modules/fake_plate.py, this module is the primary novel contribution of SVIES.

Eight independent forensic checks are applied sequentially to each detected
plate (Table I), with the forensic flow illustrated in Fig. 2.
CMVR Color Validation classifies the plate background in
HSV space against four legally prescribed CMVR categories:

Fig. 1. SVIES seven-layer processing pipeline from CCTV input to enforcement alert and dashboard output. Layers 2.5 and 4.5 are novel
sub-layers not present in any prior Indian ANPR system. TABLE I
EIGHT-MODE FAKE PLATE DETECTION CHECKS IN SVIES
#
Flag
Detection Logic
STOLEN_VEHICLE
Cross-check stolen DB
0.5
VAHAN_NOT_EXISTS
Absent from VAHAN
TYPE_MISMATCH

COLOR_CODE_VIOLATION

FONT_ANOMALY

PRIVATE (white, H ∈[0, 180], S ∈[0, 30], V ∈[200, 255]),
COMMERCIAL (yellow), EV (green), and RENTAL (black
with yellow text). IS 10731 Font Anomaly Detection: character height-to-plate ratio (expected 0.5–0.7) and inter-character
spacing ratio (expected 0.1–0.3) are evaluated; a plate is
flagged when >30% of characters violate these metrics. Clone
Detection: an in-memory dictionary keyed by plate number
stores the last-seen UTC timestamp and camera identifier; a
DUPLICATE_PLATE_CLONE alert fires when the same plate
is observed at two distinct cameras within a configurable 10minute window. D. Layer 3: OCR and Plate Parsing
The OCR pipeline (modules/ocr_parser.py) applies six preprocessing variants to each cropped plate region and selects the highest-confidence result (Fig. 3, Algorithm 1). The six variants are: Otsu binarization, CLAHE+Otsu
(clipLimit=4.0), denoised Otsu, bitwise inversion, upscaled
grayscale (250 px, INTER LANCZOS4), and adaptive Gaussian thresholding (blockSize=31, C=10). EasyOCR 1.7 with an
A–Z 0–9 whitelist serves as the primary engine; Tesseract 5.0
PSM-8 acts as fallback when EasyOCR confidence falls below threshold θlow. Position-aware character correction maps
common OCR confusions for each plate position: state code
(positions 0–1: letters only), district code (positions 2–3: digits

11: return P ∗
only), series (positions 4–5: letters), and number (positions 6–
9: digits). E. Layer 4: Multi-Database Intelligence and Risk Scoring
This layer executes threaded real-time cross-reference
queries against four databases (VAHAN, PUCC, insurance,
and stolen vehicle), with each query running in a separate
Python thread to minimise latency. The weighted risk score is
computed as:
S =

wi · 1[violationi]
! × zk
(1)
where wi is the violation weight (Table II) and zk
∈
{1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5} is the geofence zone multiplier for zone
type k (HIGHWAY, GOVT, HOSPITAL, SCHOOL respectively). Alert thresholds are: CRITICAL (S ≥61), HIGH (41–
60), MEDIUM (21–40), and LOW (≤20). The risk scoring
flow is shown in Fig. 4.
F. Layer 4.5: Helmet and Seatbelt Violation Detection
Implemented in modules/helmet_detector.py, the
detector applies a three-tier cascade. First, the custom

Fig. 3. SVIES six-variant OCR ensemble pipeline with position-aware correction, regex validation, and Groq LLM verification. TABLE II

svies_helmet_detector.pt model (YOLOv8s, trained
on 14,947 images covering two-wheeler riders in diverse
Indian road conditions) is queried at confidence threshold
0.45. If confidence is insufficient, YOLOv8n-pose keypoint
estimation localises rider head regions using the nose, left-ear,
and right-ear keypoints. As a final fallback, a skin-detection
heuristic analyses the head ROI in HSV and YCrCb color
spaces calibrated for Indian skin tones. A helmet is confirmed
absent when:
helmet absent = (ρnon-skin > 0.75) ∧(ρedge > 0.08)
(2)
where ρnon-skin is the fraction of head-ROI pixels outside the
skin color range and ρedge is the normalised Canny edge density. Seatbelt detection applies Canny edge detection (thresholds 50, 150) followed by Hough line transform (threshold=30,
minLen=40, maxGap=10); ≥3 diagonal lines in the driver-side
ROI infers seatbelt presence. G. Layers 5–7: Geofencing, Offender Intelligence, and Dashboard
The geofencing engine (modules/geofence.py) uses
Shapely point-in-polygon tests against 14 predefined Hyderabad zones (SCHOOL, HOSPITAL, GOVT, HIGHWAY,
LOW EMISSION) loaded dynamically from the Overpass
API, with multipliers 1.5, 1.4, 1.3, and 1.2 respectively. Alert
routing is level-based: CRITICAL/HIGH triggers Twilio SMS,
Gmail SMTP email, and WhatsApp notification to the nearest
police station and RTO owner; MEDIUM dispatches email to
RTO only; LOW is logged silently to the database. The repeated offender tracker maintains a per-plate 30-day
violation count in Supabase. Tier 1 (1–2 violations within 30
days) dispatches standard SMS/email to the vehicle owner and
local RTO; Tier 2 (3–5) escalates to the district traffic superintendent with a challan warning; Tier 3 (6+) auto-generates
a court summons PDF via ReportLab and red-flags the plate. Every alert record carries a SHA-256 hash computed over the

Fig. 4. Risk scoring, geofence zone multipliers, three-tier repeated offender escalation, and tiered alert dispatch in SVIES. concatenated plate number, ISO 8601 UTC timestamp, and
sorted violation list, ensuring cryptographic tamper-evidence
for legal proceedings. The React 19 dashboard (Vite 7.3)
streams violations via WebSocket, renders geofence overlays
via Leaflet, provides analytics via Recharts, and enforces
Firebase RBAC (ADMIN, POLICE, RTO, VIEWER) with
PDF and CSV export capability. IV. IMPLEMENTATION
A. Training Infrastructure and Dataset Composition
All YOLOv8s models were trained on an NVIDIA Tesla
V100-SXM2 (32 GB VRAM) using the Ultralytics API with
AdamW optimiser and a cosine learning rate schedule (Table III). Standard YOLOv8 augmentation was applied: mosaic
composition, HSV color jitter, horizontal flip, and scale variation. Training time was 3–5 hours per model on the V100. The plate detector was trained on 2,101 images merged
from two Roboflow datasets: indian-number-plate (v3, 1,683
images) and indian-cars-number-plate (v1, 418 images). The

helmet detector used 14,947 images with class remapping
into with_helmet and without_helmet. The vehicle
classifier used 12,871 images from vehicles-openimages with
11 custom class labels. The ResNet50 age classifier was
trained on 8,400 images across five age categories using
transfer learning from ImageNet pre-trained weights. B. Software Stack
The system is implemented in Python 3.10+ with PyTorch 2.0+ and Ultralytics YOLOv8 ≥8.0. OpenCV 4.8

handles frame extraction and color analysis. EasyOCR 1.7
and pytesseract 0.3.10 provide the OCR layer; the Groq
SDK 0.28 integrates Llama-4 Scout 17B vision verification. FastAPI 0.100 + Uvicorn serves the backend API; Supabase
(PostgreSQL) provides cloud-hosted persistent storage with
local SQLite fallback for offline operation. V. RESULTS AND EVALUATION
A. Model Detection Performance
Table IV presents performance metrics on held-out test sets. The plate detector achieves precision 0.988 and mAP@50
of 97.7%, significantly exceeding the 95% design target. The mAP@50-95 of 73.0% indicates robust localisation under stricter IoU thresholds, confirming suitability for tight
bounding-box crops fed to the OCR engine. The helmet detector reaches 74.4% mAP@50 with recall 0.733, reflecting the
inherent challenge of detecting helmet absence under variable
pose, rider distance, and partial occlusion by handlebars. The
vehicle classifier achieves 64.4% mAP@50; lower recall of
0.584 is attributable to inter-class visual similarity between
AUTO, TEMPO, and E RICKSHAW at typical CCTV resolutions. All models achieve sub-3.1 ms per-image inference
on GPU, supporting real-time processing at 25 FPS. B. Dashboard and System Output
Figure 5 shows the SVIES React 19 dashboard across its
four primary views. TABLE V

C. Feature Comparison
D. OCR Performance
The six-variant ensemble with Groq LLM verification
achieves approximately 99.8% character-level accuracy on
standard Indian plates under normal daytime conditions, degrading to approximately 96.2% on weathered or partially
occluded plates where LLM correction contributes most.

The position-aware correction module reduces digit-for-letter
confusions (e.g., 0↔O, 1↔I, 8↔B) by an estimated 78%
compared to raw EasyOCR output on the held-out test set. BHseries plate parsing (format: YY BH NNNN X) is validated
with a dedicated regex branch and achieves 99.1% formatcompliance accuracy. Fig 5 Shows you the live detection
working and Dashboard.
VI. DISCUSSION
A. Novelty and Contributions
The eight-mode fake plate forensics pipeline is the first
system targeting Indian ANPR to unify database-level verification (VAHAN existence, stolen status, PUCC, insurance)
with structural forensics (CMVR color, IS 10731 font metrics)
and spatio-temporal fraud detection (clone detection across
cameras). The LLM-augmented OCR layer represents a novel
application of large-language-model vision capabilities to the
ANPR domain: by passing the plate crop and initial OCR

string to Llama-4 Scout via the Groq inference API, the
system corrects residual character errors on partially occluded,
weathered, or damaged plates that defeat all six preprocessing
variants. The hybrid helmet detector’s skin-tone heuristic, calibrated using HSV and YCrCb color spaces (see (2)), directly
addresses a known limitation of standard helmet detectors
trained primarily on non-Indian datasets where skin-tone distributions differ significantly. The zero-cost, fully open-source
stack (YOLOv8, EasyOCR, FastAPI, React, Supabase free tier,
OpenStreetMap/Overpass) makes SVIES viable for Tier-2 and
Tier-3 city municipalities that cannot afford commercial ANPR
licensing fees. B. Limitations and Deployment Considerations
The primary limitation is the use of mock JSON databases
in place of live VAHAN, PUCC, and police API connections,
which requires formal access agreements with the Ministry
of Road Transport and Highways. The vehicle classifier’s
recall of 0.584 reflects limited training data for Indianspecific classes; expansion with a larger annotated corpus is
planned. Clone detection currently operates in-memory per
server instance, without cross-server synchronisation; a Redisbacked distributed dictionary is identified as the immediate
next step. As demonstrated in [15], live camera feeds can
be compromised by EMI attacks; future deployments should
integrate EMI-hardened camera sensor modules [14].
VII. CONCLUSION
This paper presented SVIES, a seven-layer AI-powered
vehicle enforcement system addressing critical gaps in Indian
road enforcement. Three custom YOLOv8s models achieve
97.7% mAP@50 (plate, precision 0.988), 74.4% mAP@50
(helmet), and 64.4% mAP@50 (vehicle). The six-variant
LLM-augmented OCR pipeline achieves approximately 99.8%
character accuracy on standard Indian plates. The eightmode fake plate forensics module, hybrid helmet detector,
cryptographic evidence chain, and offline edge mode collectively constitute the most comprehensive open-source Indian
ANPR enforcement platform to date. Future directions: (i) live
VAHAN/police API integration via government partnerships;
(ii) IS 10731 CNN training corpus expansion; (iii) federated
learning across distributed edge nodes; (iv) optical-flow overspeeding detection; (v) Redis-backed distributed clone detection; (vi) EMI-hardened camera modules [14], [15] for resilience in adversarial deployment environments. The complete
codebase will be open-sourced on GitHub upon publication. REFERENCES
